cards.grammarNote: V stem + 고 connects two actions. 일하다 (to work) → 일하고. The second verb carries the tense for the whole sentence.

cards.front: I read and listen to music.
cards.grammarNote: 읽다 (to read) → 읽고. -고 can also list simultaneous or sequential activities. 음악을 듣다 = to listen to music (ㄷ irregular: 듣 → 들).

cards.front: I wake up and drink coffee.
cards.grammarNote: 일어나다 (to wake up/get up) → 일어나고. With -고, the sequence is implied: first A, then B.
노래하다 (to sing) → 노래하고. 춤을 추다 = to dance (contracted: 추 + 어요 → 춰요).
요리하다 (to cook) → 요리하고. Simple sequential actions. -고 is neutral and works with any two verbs.

cards.front: I finish work and go to the gym.
cards.grammarNote: 마치다 (to finish) → 마치고. 헬스장 = gym. -고 clearly marks the sequence: first finish work, then go.

cards.front: My hobby is painting and drawing.
cards.grammarNote: Here -하고 connects two nouns (painting and drawing) as 'and'. -하고 can connect nouns, while -고 connects verbs.

cards.front: I exercise and take a shower.
cards.grammarNote: 운동하다 (to exercise) → 운동하고. 샤워하다 (to shower) → 샤워해요. Both are 하다 verbs, making -고 easy to apply.

cards.front: He plays guitar and writes songs.
cards.grammarNote: 치다 (to play an instrument) → 치고. 쓰다 (to write) → 써요. -고 works the same regardless of verb class.

cards.front: I meet friends and eat out.
cards.grammarNote: 만나다 (to meet) → 만나고. 외식하다 = to eat out (외식 = eating out). Sequential social activity.

cards.front: I study Korean and watch dramas.
cards.grammarNote: 공부하다 (to study) → 공부하고. 보다 (to watch/see) → 봐요 (contracted). Both are everyday hobby actions.
